---
title: "A Tensor Is a Flat Buffer and a Promise: Strides, Views, and Contiguity"
description: "Most tensor ops don't touch your data — they hand you new metadata over the same memory. Understanding that one fact turns view/reshape/permute from incantations into arithmetic, and explains the class of bugs that produce the right shape and the wrong numbers."
author: "Maitreya Suin"
categories: [fundamentals, tensors]
date: 2026-08-04
depth: "Deep dive"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
---

> **TL;DR** — A tensor is a 1-D block of memory plus `(shape, strides, offset)`. `view`, `transpose`, `permute`, `expand` and slicing don't move a single byte; they hand you new metadata over the same buffer. Internalize that and three things follow: why `view` fails after `transpose`, why `reshape` can silently cost you a memcpy, and — the one that actually breaks models — why splitting a feature axis the wrong way gives you **the correct shape and the wrong tensor, with no error.**

## Why this comes up

Every tensor interview question is secretly the same question: *can you reason about memory layout?* The `view`-vs-`reshape` trivia is the warm-up. The real probe is the one that comes ten minutes later, when they hand you a packed feature axis and ask you to split it into heads — and the wrong answer runs fine, returns the shape you promised, and quietly corrupts your model.

I want to spend most of this post on that second kind.

## Build it: the three-number model

A tensor is **not** a nested array. It's a flat buffer plus three pieces of metadata:

- **shape** — the size of each dimension
- **strides** — how many *elements* you skip in the flat buffer to advance one step along each dimension
- **storage offset** — where in the buffer this tensor starts

(This is the model for **dense strided tensors** — which is everything in this post, and ~99% of the tensors you'll ever touch. Sparse, quantized, and nested tensors carry different metadata; don't let that footnote stop you from internalizing the dense case.)

Element `x[i, j, k]` lives at:

$$\text{flat} = \text{offset} + i \cdot s_0 + j \cdot s_1 + k \cdot s_2$$

That's it. That's the whole model. You can check it by hand:

```{python}
import torch
x = torch.arange(24, dtype=torch.float32).reshape(2, 3, 4)

print("shape  ", tuple(x.shape))
print("strides", x.stride())          # (12, 4, 1)
print("contig ", x.is_contiguous())

i, j, k = 1, 2, 3
flat = x.storage_offset() + i*x.stride(0) + j*x.stride(1) + k*x.stride(2)
print(f"\nx[{i},{j},{k}]      = {x[i,j,k].item()}")
print(f"hand-computed flat = {flat}")
print(f"buffer[{flat}]      = {x.flatten()[flat].item()}")
```

```
shape   (2, 3, 4)
strides (12, 4, 1)
contig  True

x[1,2,3]      = 23.0
hand-computed flat = 23
buffer[23]      = 23.0
```

Strides `(12, 4, 1)` for shape `(2,3,4)`: to move one step along the last axis, skip 1 element. Along the middle axis, skip 4 (a whole row). Along the first, skip 12 (a whole 3×4 slab). The last dimension is the **fastest-varying** — neighbours in the last axis are neighbours in memory. That's what "C-contiguous" means, and it's the layout every freshly created tensor has.

**Now the payoff.** `transpose` doesn't move data. It swaps two strides:

```{python}
y = x.transpose(1, 2)                 # shape (2, 4, 3)

print("x.stride() ", x.stride())
print("y.stride() ", y.stride())      # the 4 and the 1 traded places
print("y contiguous?", y.is_contiguous())
print("same memory? ", y.data_ptr() == x.data_ptr())
```

```
x.stride()  (12, 4, 1)
y.stride()  (12, 1, 4)
y contiguous? False
same memory?  True
```

Same buffer. Different lens. Zero bytes copied. This is why `transpose` and `permute` are effectively free — and why the tensor they hand back is **non-contiguous**: its strides are no longer decreasing, so walking the last axis no longer walks memory in order.

### The rule that follows

- **`view`** reinterprets the buffer with a new shape. It **requires** a compatible layout and **never copies**. If it can't, it raises.
- **`reshape`** is `view` if possible, `contiguous().view()` otherwise. It never fails *for layout reasons* — the element count must still match (`torch.arange(24).reshape(5, 7)` raises) — and *therefore* it can silently allocate and memcpy. One more subtlety the docs are explicit about: you should **not** rely on whether any particular successful `reshape` returned a view or a copy. If aliasing matters to your code, check `data_ptr()` yourself, exactly as we do below.
- **`contiguous()`** materializes a properly-laid-out copy. On an already-contiguous tensor it's a no-op.

```{python}
#| error: true
y.view(-1)      # y came from a transpose — non-contiguous
```

```
RuntimeError: view size is not compatible with input tensor's size and stride
(at least one dimension spans across two contiguous subspaces).
Use .reshape(...) instead.
```

```{python}
z = y.reshape(-1)                     # works — but at what price?
print("reshape succeeded. shares memory with x?", z.data_ptr() == x.data_ptr())
```

```
reshape succeeded. shares memory with x? False
```

`False` means it copied. `view` shouted at you; `reshape` quietly charged you a memcpy. Neither is wrong — but you should know which one you're paying for. **Use `view` when you want a hard guarantee of no copy**; use `reshape` when you don't care and want it to just work.

### `expand` vs `repeat`: the stride-0 trick

The same idea, taken to its logical extreme. To broadcast a `(3,1)` tensor to `(3,4)`, you don't need four copies of each value — you need a stride of **zero**:

```{python}
a = torch.arange(3.).view(3, 1)
e = a.expand(3, 4)      # broadcast view
r = a.repeat(1, 4)      # physical copy

print("a.stride()          ", a.stride())
print("a.expand(3,4).stride()", e.stride(), "  <- stride 0 on the broadcast axis")
print("a.repeat(1,4).stride()", r.stride())
print("\nexpand shares storage?", e.data_ptr() == a.data_ptr())
print("repeat shares storage?", r.data_ptr() == a.data_ptr())
print("values identical?     ", torch.equal(e, r))
```

```
a.stride()           (1, 1)
a.expand(3,4).stride() (1, 0)   <- stride 0 on the broadcast axis
a.repeat(1,4).stride() (4, 1)

expand shares storage? True
repeat shares storage? False
values identical?      True
```

Stride 0 means *"advancing along this axis doesn't move in memory at all"* — every column reads the same element. It's a lie the metadata tells, and it's free. `repeat` tells the truth and pays for it. For read-only broadcasting, `expand` every time.

## Break it: the same shape, the wrong tensor

Here's the bug that matters. You're given a packed feature axis and asked to split it into per-head queries and keys:

```python
def get_qk(x, nh, hd):
    """
    x: (B, S, D) where D = nh * 2 * hd
    The feature axis is packed per-head, INTERLEAVED:
        [h0_q, h0_k, h1_q, h1_k, ..., h_{n}_q, h_{n}_k]
    Return q, k each of shape (B, nh, S, hd).
    """
```

The layout tells you the index arithmetic: feature slot for head `h`, part `qk ∈ {0,1}`, dim `d` is

$$\text{feature} = h \cdot (2 \cdot hd) + qk \cdot hd + d$$

That is *exactly* the flattening of `(nh, 2, hd)` with `nh` slowest and `hd` fastest. So the split is `view(B, S, nh, 2, hd)`. Now watch what three plausible-looking answers do:

```{python}
import torch
torch.manual_seed(0)
B, S, nh, hd = 2, 5, 4, 8
D = nh * 2 * hd
x = torch.randn(B, S, D)

def ground_truth(x, nh, hd):
    """Brute force, straight from the spec. Slow, obviously correct."""
    B, S, _ = x.shape
    q = torch.zeros(B, nh, S, hd); k = torch.zeros(B, nh, S, hd)
    for h in range(nh):
        q[:, h] = x[:, :, h*2*hd       : h*2*hd + hd]
        k[:, h] = x[:, :, h*2*hd + hd  : h*2*hd + 2*hd]
    return q, k

def correct(x, nh, hd):
    B, S, _ = x.shape
    x = x.view(B, S, nh, 2, hd)                    # nh slowest, hd fastest
    return x[:, :, :, 0, :].transpose(1, 2), x[:, :, :, 1, :].transpose(1, 2)

def wrong_chunk(x, nh, hd):
    B, S, _ = x.shape
    q, k = x.chunk(2, dim=-1)                      # assumes [all_Q | all_K]
    return (q.view(B, S, nh, hd).transpose(1, 2),
            k.view(B, S, nh, hd).transpose(1, 2))

def wrong_order(x, nh, hd):
    B, S, _ = x.shape
    x = x.view(B, S, 2, nh, hd)                    # split order flipped
    return x[:, :, 0].transpose(1, 2), x[:, :, 1].transpose(1, 2)

gq, gk = ground_truth(x, nh, hd)
for name, fn in [("correct  view(B,S,nh,2,hd)", correct),
                 ("WRONG    chunk(2, dim=-1) ", wrong_chunk),
                 ("WRONG    view(B,S,2,nh,hd)", wrong_order)]:
    q, k = fn(x, nh, hd)
    print(f"{name} | shape {tuple(q.shape)} | matches truth: {torch.equal(q, gq)}")
```

```
correct  view(B,S,nh,2,hd) | shape (2, 4, 5, 8) | matches truth: True
WRONG    chunk(2, dim=-1)  | shape (2, 4, 5, 8) | matches truth: False
WRONG    view(B,S,2,nh,hd) | shape (2, 4, 5, 8) | matches truth: False
```

**Read that output again.** All three return `(2, 4, 5, 8)`. All three run without a warning. Two of them return a tensor whose max element-wise deviation from the truth is `4.74` — i.e. completely different numbers. Your assertion on `q.shape` passes. Your model trains. It just trains worse than it should, forever, and you never find out.

This is why layout bugs are the ones to fear. A shape bug throws. A layout bug ships.

The two wrong answers correspond to two *other, equally real* packings:

| Feature-axis layout | Correct split |
|---|---|
| `[h0_q, h0_k, h1_q, h1_k, …]` — interleaved per head | `view(B,S,nh,2,hd)`, index `[...,0,:]` / `[...,1,:]` |
| `[all_Q (nh·hd) ‖ all_K (nh·hd)]` — block-packed | `chunk(2,-1)`, then each `view(B,S,nh,hd)` |
| `[Q-block ‖ K-block ‖ V-block]` — fused QKV (nanoGPT) | `view(B,S,3,nh,hd)`, index `[:,:,0/1/2]` |

Real codebases use all three. **So the correct first move in the interview is not to write code — it's to ask "how is the feature axis packed?"** Asking that question is the entire signal.

## Check it: brute force is a testing tool, not a shameful first draft

The `ground_truth` function above is the most valuable code in this post. It's a slow, ugly, obviously-correct Python loop transcribed directly from the index formula in the spec. It's *unusable* in production — a kernel launch per head, no vectorization. And it is exactly what you should write first, because it pins the semantics down where you can't argue with them.

Then you write the fast version and assert they're equal. This is the whole method:

```{python}
q, k = correct(x, nh, hd)
assert torch.equal(q, gq) and torch.equal(k, gk)
print("vectorized == brute force  ✓")
```

Two more ways to say the same thing, both worth knowing:

```{python}
from einops import rearrange
qk = rearrange(x, 'b s (nh two hd) -> b s nh two hd', nh=nh, two=2, hd=hd)
q_ein = rearrange(qk[..., 0, :], 'b s nh hd -> b nh s hd')
print("einops matches:", torch.equal(q_ein, gq))
```

```
einops matches: True
```

The einops version is the one I'd actually ship, and not because it's shorter. The pattern string `(nh two hd)` **is the layout assumption, written down**. The bug we just walked through is *unrepresentable* in einops, because einops forces you to name the axes in memory order. That's a real argument for the library, and it's stronger than "it's more readable."

## Worked example: patchify, and where the copy hides

Same recipe, harder shape. Turn `(N, C, H, W)` into `(N, C·p², H/p, W/p)` — the front of every ViT, and `nn.PixelUnshuffle`:

```{python}
N, C, H, W, ps = 2, 3, 8, 8, 2
img = torch.randn(N, C, H, W)
hp, wp = H // ps, W // ps

out = (img
       .reshape(N, C, hp, ps, wp, ps)     # 1. split H -> (hp, ps), W -> (wp, ps)
       .permute(0, 1, 3, 5, 2, 4)         # 2. -> (N, C, ps, ps, hp, wp)
       .reshape(N, C*ps*ps, hp, wp))      # 3. fold (C, ps, ps) into channels

print(tuple(img.shape), "->", tuple(out.shape))
print("matches nn.PixelUnshuffle?", torch.equal(out, torch.nn.PixelUnshuffle(ps)(img)))
```

```
(2, 3, 8, 8) -> (2, 12, 4, 4)
matches nn.PixelUnshuffle? True
```

Step 1 works because a row index `r ∈ [0,H)` decomposes as `r = i·ps + ih` — patch-row `i` slow, within-patch offset `ih` fast — which is precisely how `reshape` splits a contiguous axis. Step 2 moves the within-patch offsets next to the channel axis. Step 3 folds them in.

But notice what step 2 did to the memory:

```{python}
step2 = img.reshape(N, C, hp, ps, wp, ps).permute(0, 1, 3, 5, 2, 4)
print("after permute, contiguous?", step2.is_contiguous())
print("final reshape shares storage with img?",
      step2.reshape(N, C*ps*ps, hp, wp).data_ptr() == img.data_ptr())
```

```
after permute, contiguous? False
final reshape shares storage with img? False
```

The final `reshape` **must** copy — there is no stride assignment that expresses the new layout over the old buffer, and `view` would (correctly) refuse. That copy isn't a bug; patchify genuinely rearranges data. But it *is* a cost, and it's the kind of cost that hides in a data pipeline and shows up as an unexplained plateau in your GPU utilization graph.

The channel ordering that falls out is `c·p² + ih·p + iw` — channel-major. That is **a** convention, not **the** convention. A different `permute` gives a different, equally valid ordering. If you're loading pretrained weights, you must match theirs.

## The follow-up

**"So when would you deliberately use `view` over `reshape`?"** When a copy would be a bug, not just a cost — inside a hot decode loop, or where you're about to mutate the result and expect it to write through to the original buffer. `view` is an assertion. Use it as one.

**"What's `channels_last`?"** A different stride ordering for `(N,C,H,W)` — physically `(N,H,W,C)` while *reporting* shape `(N,C,H,W)`. Same shape, same API, different memory layout, and it's what Tensor Cores want for convolutions. It's the cleanest proof that shape and layout are genuinely independent: `x.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)` changes zero shapes and every stride.

**"Why does the last axis being stride-1 matter?"** Coalescing. When 32 threads in a warp each read one element, the hardware can service them in one transaction *if* those elements are adjacent in memory. Non-contiguous access serializes it. That's the bridge from this post to the GPU post — and it's the reason `.contiguous()` isn't just bookkeeping.

## References

- [PyTorch: Tensor Views](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensor_view.html) — the authoritative list of which ops return views. Worth reading once, all the way through; it's shorter than you'd think.
- [`torch.Tensor.stride`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.stride.html) and [`is_contiguous`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.is_contiguous.html).
- [einops](https://einops.rocks/) — Rogozhnikov's library. The pitch isn't brevity; it's that the layout assumption becomes a string you can read.
- [PyTorch: Channels Last memory format](https://pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/memory_format_tutorial.html).
- Karpathy, [nanoGPT](https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT) — see `CausalSelfAttention` for the fused-QKV block packing, the third row of the table above.
